State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662        322-5660              322-5901         322-6441           322-6444

January 26, 1983


Norman Flete
Deputy Attorney General
Department of Justice
3580 Wilshire Blvd.
Los Angeles, CA 90010

Re: Los Angeles Coliseum - Raiders
Tickets
Our File No. A-82-211

Dear Mr. Flete:

You have requested a clarification of one portion of our recent letter to Los Angeles City Councilman Hal Bernson (No. A-82-211). You are the Deputy Attorney General assigned to advise the State's participants on the Coliseum Commission. You have provided the following supplemental facts:

The State's portion of the triumvirate Coliseum Commission ("Coliseum") is the 6th Agricultural District (The California Museum of Science and Industry). The Board of the 6th Agricultural District is appointed by the Governor to fixed terms. This Board, in turn, selects from its own membership its representatives on the Coliseum.

I have advised you that, based upon these facts, the officials of the 6th Agricultural District would be treated the same as city and county officials.* Thus, the distribution of free tickets to the Raiders games, by the Coliseum (as described in the Bernson letter) to 6th Agricultural District officials would be considered part of their compensation and thus not

*Officials here include all high-ranking city and county officials, such as treasurer, city attorney, county counsel, etc.

Norman Fleete
January 26, 1983
Page Two


gift. Contrary to what the Benson letter stated, this is not
limited to the 6th Agricultural District officials who actually
sit on the Coliseum Commission.

I trust that this clarifies the matter to your satisfaction.

Sincerely,

Robert E. Leidigh
Counsel
Legal Division

REL:plh
cc: Anthony Saul Alperin
    Thomas Bonaventura
    Deputy City Attorneys

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662          322-5660             322-5901            322-6441              322-6444


January 19, 1983


Hal Bernson
Councilmember, 12th District
City of Los Angeles
City Hall
Los Angeles, CA 90012

                                                Re: Your Request for Written
                                                    Advice, Our No. A-82-211

Dear Councilmember Bernson:

     Thank you for your recent letter requesting our advice
regarding complimentary tickets to Raiders football games
at the Los Angeles Coliseum. We have previously received
telephone inquiries from Anthony Saul Alperin of the City
Attorney's Office regarding the issue and have rendered
advice to him on the subject. However, it was unclear to
him at that time who, in fact, was the provider of the
tickets. You state the facts in your letter as follows:

                              FACTS

     A question has been raised regarding the receipt
of tickets from the Los Angeles Raiders football team
by my office. I would appreciate your ruling on
whether or not I now have a conflict of interest in
matters dealing with the Los Angeles Memorial Coliseum
and the Los Angeles Raiders.

     On September 1, 1982, my office received four
season tickets to Raiders games which were
scheduled to be played in the Los Angeles Memorial
Coliseum. (The City of Los Angeles is a one-third
partner with the County of Los Angeles and the State of
California in the administration of the Coliseum.) The
first tickets were for a game played on August 29,
1982, three days prior to receipt.

Hal Bernson
January 19, 1983
Page 2


     The tickets for the games to be played on
December 18, and December 26, 1982, have not been used
and have been returned.

     The games scheduled for October 3, 10, 31 and
November 7, 1982, were cancelled because of the NFL
players' strike.

     The tickets for the games played on September 4,
November 22, and December 5, 1982, were given to
constituents and I have subsequently sent a check to
pay for them.

                         QUESTION

     My request is, "Does the conflict of interest
occur upon receipt of the tickets (September 1), or for
the use of tickets, i.e., September 4, November 22, and
December 5, which value is $216.00, under the $250
limitation?"  The balance of tickets were returned to
the Raiders on December 15, 1982, with a check for
$216.

                         ANALYSIS

     Generally, the Commission's advice has been that if
free tickets or passes are received by city officials to
events held at a municipal stadium where the seats were
reserved for the city's use as a part of the stadium lease
agreement, then there is no gift made to the officials
because the tickets come from the city and are merely part
of the "compensation" for the official. This advise was
originally given in 1979 in response to a request from
John W. Witt, City Attorney for San Diego.

     When Mr. Alperin contacted us in July, we were advised
that the Los Angeles Coliseum Commission (hereinafter the
"Coliseum") operates the Los Angeles Memorial Coliseum as a
joint powers agency between the City of Los Angeles, the
County of Los Angeles and the State of California (the oth
Agricultural District, which is the Museum of Science and
Industry). As part of its lease agreements, the Coliseum
routinely requires the lessee to set aside a certain number
of tickets to the lessee's events which are then distri-
buted according to a list prepared by the Coliseum. The
list consists of public officials who receive either two or
four tickets to each event, depending on how many the

Hal Bernson
January 19, 1983
Page 3


Coliseum decides to allocate to each specific official.
The tickets are then provided and distributed by the lessee
to this list of public officials. We were told to assume
that the lessee has no veto power over the selections,1/
out does have discretion over seat location.2/

We advised Mr. Alperin that, since the Coliseum is a
triumvirate comprised of the City, County and State, the
free tickets to City officials would be deemed to be part
of the compensation which they received from the City. The
same is true for County officials as well as for City and
County staff actually assigned to the Coliseum. However,
for state officials, only those who are actually members of
the Coliseum, would fall into this category, along with
state staff assigned to the Coliseum. As compensation from
a public entity, the tickets do not represent reportable
income and, hence, raise no conflict of interest problems.

In response to our inquiries following receipt of your
letter, we are now advised by Mr. Alperin and his
colleague, Tom Bonaventura, the deputy city attorney
assigned to the Coliseum, of the following additional
facts.

1. The Coliseum adopted a policy in August 1973
relative to the issue of complimentary tickets to be
distributed by lessees for Coliseum events. (See Exhibit
"A".) General complimentary tickets to be distributed at
the sole discretion of the lessee are limited to a maximum
of 3,000 per event. This is to protect the Coliseum's
interests since its revenues are based upon a


1/ Mr. Alperin indicated that it was possible that
the Coliseum merely prepares a "snopping list" of names of
public officials, and the lessee may then select among
those names in determining who will receive the free
tickets (some 200+). Given that this was a speculative
version of the facts, we advised Mr. Alperin on the basis
of the assumption that the Coliseum actually selects the
recipients of the tickets.

2/ In point of fact, you have received "very good"
seats according to the Coliseum. All of the 256 seats
provided by the Raiders are in good locations.

Hal Bernson
January 19, 1983
Page 4


percentage of the gate receipts. In addition to the
foregoing allotment, the policy provides for a maximum of
340 additional complimentary tickets to be distributed by
the lessee to local governmental officials. (See Exhibit
"A".) A list is provided by the Coliseum. The list
consists of local public officials, including Coliseum
staff, and assorted political figures. Each official is
alloted either four tickets or two tickets, according to
the list. As new officials are elected or appointed to
positions, the names on the list change. The list is
periodically reviewed and revised by the Coliseum.
Currently, the revised list totals 318, although at one
point it was down to 300.

     2.  Both of the limits for complimentary tickets
contained in the Coliseum policy are negotiable in the
lease agreements. For instance, when the Rams were the
lessee, one year they wanted to cut back on the number of
complimentary tickets which they were required to hand out
to public officials and politicians. In the preliminary
lease contract ("Agreement" - see Exhibit "B") entered into
between the Coliseum and the Raiders for the 1982 season,
the Raiders negotiated the number of required complimentary
tickets down to 236. The Agreement calls for the Raiders
to provide to the Coliseum 236 tickets for each Raiders
home game. The tickets shall be provided in such locations
as the Raiders may determine; however, once the 150 suites
are constructed by the Raiders, three suites shall be
included in the allotment to the Coliseum.

     3.  The agreement between the Raiders and the Coliseum
was reached in July 1982, and the pre-season games were
scheduled to begin in August. Consequently, time frames
were very short and ticket problems were prevalent.
Ms. Margaret Farnum, Assistant Secretary to the Coliseum,
had several conversations with Mr. Al LoCasale, a chief
Raiders assistant, regarding the need for the Raiders to
make good on their contractual obligation to provide the
complimentary tickets for the public officials in a timely
fashion. Finally, Mr. LoCasale advised Ms. Farnum that the
Raiders could not accomplish the task of distributing these
tickets. He then delivered a box to Ms. Farnum's office
containing 236 sets of season tickets to all of the
scheduled home games of the Raiders. The task of

Hal Bernson
January 19, 1983
Page 5


distribution was turned over to her assistant, who pared
down the list of 318 to 236 and distributed the tickets to
that selected group,2/ first removing one ticket for an
exhibition game which had already been played. You were
one of the recipients of these tickets. Ms. Farnum's
assistant, with her input, determined the location of the
tickets distributed to the various recipients.

ANALYSIS

You received the tickets on September 1, 1982. The
value of them is the fair market value (see our Hopkins
Opinion, 3 FPPC Opinions 107, No. 77-022, December 8, 1977,
copy enclosed). In the case of these Raiders tickets, that
is $18 each. However, since the August 29, 1982, game was
played prior to receipt of the tickets by you, the value of
tickets to that game will not be counted in the total. At
the time you received the tickets, there were nine home
games remaining on the Raiders schedule. Thus the value of
the tickets received was 9 x 4 x $18, which equals $648.
However, because of the intervening strike, that value was
reduced to $360. Therefore, if the tickets were a


_______________________

3/ Some of the recipients returned the tickets,
unused, within 30 days of receipt. Ms. Farnum kept these
tickets for others on the list who hadn't received any.
Unclaimed tickets were distributed to Coliseum staff, none
were returned to the Raiders. Some recipients paid for all
or a portion of the tickets within 30 days of receipt.
Checks were made payable to the Raiders, but were sent to
Ms. Farnum, who then forwarded them to the Raiders. She
had advised at least some of the recipients that the checks
should be made payable to the Raiders, because she assumed
that the Raiders were the donors. Written communications
were sent by the Coliseum to its members and staff and
subsequently to all recipients. These are attached as
Exhibit "C". The advice and arithmetic contained therein
is not necessarily accurate.

Hal Bernson
January 19, 1983
Page 6


gift4/ from the Raiders, both the disclosure5/ and
disqualification6/ provisions of the Act would be
applicable.

     Under the circumstances, we do not think that the
Raiders were a donor of the tickets. We limit our


4/ Government Code Section 82028, which defines the
term "gift" for purposes of the Political Reform Act,
provides, in part, that:

     "Gift" means, except as provided in subdivision
(b), any payment to the extent that consideration of
equal or greater value is not received and includes a
rebate or discount in the price of anything of value
unless the rebate or discount is made in the regular
course of business to members of the public without
regard to official status....

     Since you did not return the tickets within 30 days, the
exception in Section 82028 (see below) does not apply.

     (b)  The term "gift" does not include:

                         * * *

     (2)Gifts which are not used and which, within
30 days after receipt, are returned to the donor
or delivered to a charitable organization without
being claimed as a charitable contribution for tax
purposes;

     5/ Receipt of gifts aggregating $50 or more in value
from any one source during the reporting period must be
disclosed on an official's Statement of Economic
Interests. Government Code Section 37207(a)(1).

     6/ A gift is income under the Political Reform Act.
Section 82030(a). A source of income of $250 or more
constitutes a financial interest. Sections 87100,
87103(c). A conflict of interest requiring disqualifi-
cation could only arise as to those governmental decisions
which would foreseeably have a material financial effect
upon the Raiders. Other decisions could have been made by
you even if the Raiders had been a source of income to you.

Hal Bernson
January 19, 1983
Page 7


Conclusion to the precise facts of this situation.  The
Raiders were required by the lease agreement to supply the
236 tickets.  However, the Coliseum exercised all the
discretionary control over the distribution of these
tickets.  The Coliseum decided which tickets, and how many
of them, went to each individual official.  Consequently,
as per our previous advice to Mr. Alperin, the Coliseum is
to be considered the source of the tickets in this
situation.  The Raiders merely fulfilled a contractual
obligation which was placed upon them by the Coliseum as
part of the overall contract.  Had the Raiders wished to
donate complimentary tickets to various officials, they
clearly had a sufficient allotment of their own from which
to do so.

     In the future, if discretion as to the recipients of
these complimentary tickets is vested in the Raiders (or
any other lessee), then they are the donor and the tickets
are a gift.  In that situation, receipt of $250 or more in
tickets which are neither returned nor paid for within 30
days would create the basis for a conflict of interest/
on the part of a public official who receives such a gift.

     I trust that this letter has satisfactorily responded to
your question.  Should you have any questions regarding this
advice, please contact me at 916/322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Robert E. Beidigh
                                        Counsel
                                        Legal Division

     City Attorney's Office

_____________________________

     1/  Id.

EXHIBIT "A"


COMPLIMENTARY TICKETS

The policy of the Commission in regard to complimentary tickets
shall be as follows:

1. The standard form for contracts shall include a paragraph to
the effect that the licensee shall not authorize free admissions in
excess of 3,000 at the Coliseum, 500 at the Sports Arena without
the written approval of the Licensee.

2. Complimentary tickets issued to local officials of the City,
County and State shall be issued and distributed through the licensee.
The number shall not exceed 300 in the Coliseum and 118 at the Sports
Arena and shall be in addition to the tickets provided for in paragraph
(1) above.

J STAR KEVIN
ROBERT D SELLECK

LOS ANGELES MEMORIAL COLISEUM COMMISSION

COLISEUM, 3911 SO. FIGUEROA ST. • 747-7111 — SPORTS ARENA, 3939 S. FIGUEROA ST. • 748-6131

LOS ANGELES, CALIFORNIA 90037

January 3, 1983

TO:     Recipients of Complimentary Tickets to Los Angeles Raiders
        Football Games

FROM:   Los Angeles Coliseum Commission

RE:     Conflict of Interest and Disclosure Code

The Los Angeles Raiders have turned over to us to distribute to the
list of those who receive complimentary tickets the NFL Playoff
Tickets for games to be played in the Los Angeles Coliseum on
January 8, January 15 or 16, and January 22 or 23. It is possible
that not all three games will be played, if the Raiders are not
successful in the first two games.

Enclosed are the complimentary tickets in the same location as your
regular tickets. Although these are normally complimentary tickets,
if you have received two complimentary tickets for the regular and pre-
season games, your total gift, to date, is $216.00. Since the package
of three playoff tickets is $18.00, $20.00, and $23.00, the cost of
two sets of playoff tickets would be $122.00. To keep you under the
$250.00 limit, a check should be sent to the Los Angeles Raiders in
care of the Coliseum office in the amount of $89.00.

If you received four complimentary tickets, that total would have been
$432.00. However, many have sent checks to the Los Angeles Raiders
in the amount of $183.00 to bring them under the $250.00 gift limit.
The enclosed package of four playoff ticket sets is valued at $244.00.
It is recommended that checks in the full amount of $244.00 be sent
to the Los Angeles Raiders in care of the Los Angeles Coliseum office.

Because of the thirty-day limit, it is recommended that checks should
be in our office by February 1, 1983. If the returned tickets prior
to the game being played. Since the last game will be January 22 or
23, you will know the value of the gift and for how much the check
should be made, deducting those amounts for games that may not be
played.

If you have any questions, please call (747-7111).

Sincerely,

[illegible]

OFFICERS
M J FRANKOVICH
PRESIDENT

GILBERT W LINDSAY

JAMES F HARDY

ASSISTANT MANAGER

STATE OF CALIFORNIA
WILLIAM CAMPBELL
MJ FRANKOVICH
WILLIAM H ROBERTSON

WALTER RASIC
ALTERNATE

COUNTY OF LOS ANGELES
MICHAEL ANTONOVICH
DEANE DANA
PETER SCHABARUM

EDMUND EDELMAN
ALTERNATE

CITY OF LOS ANGELES
GILBERT W LINDSAY
JOEL W GIBSON
ROBERT D SELLECK

LOS ANGELES MEMORIAL COLISEUM COMMISSION
COLISEUM, 3911 SO. FIGUEROA ST. • 747-7111 — SPORTS ARENA, 3939 S. FIGUEROA ST. • 748-6131
LOS ANGELES, CALIFORNIA 90037

JOHN FERRARO
IRVING ZEIGER
ALTERNATES

December 9, 1982

TO:     Recipients of Complimentary Tickets to Los Angeles Raiders
        Football Games and University of Southern California Football
        and Basketball Games

FROM:   Los Angeles Coliseum Commission


Under the Conflict of Interest and Disclosure Code, pursuant to
the Political Reform Act of 1974, public officials who receive
gifts from any one person or organization, totaling $250.00 or
more in value, may not participate in any business or decisions
involving that person or organization for a period of twelve
months from the date of receipt of said gifts.

If you received Raiders or USC sports tickets and did not return or
pay for a portion of those tickets by the legal deadline, so that
the value of what you accepted was $250.00 or more, you are pro-
hibited from voting on any business coming before you that might
deal with those organizations.  The legal deadline is thirty days
from the receipt of tickets, not counting the period of the NFL
strike.

The original face value of 1982 Raiders' season football tickets
was $180.00.  Four games were not played because of the NFL strike,
which reduces the value to $102.00.  If you received four compli-
mentary season tickets, the total value is $430.00.  To bring it
under the $250.00 limit, a check in the amount of $183.00, made
payable to the Los Angeles Raiders, should be forwarded to our offices,
if the 30-day period has not already expired.

It is my understanding that the NFL strike was called on September
20, 1982 and ended on November 21, 1982, and that the 30-day period
was tolled since such strike.  In order to know if you may return
or pay for a portion of the tickets, and thus avoid disqualification,
you need to calculate whether the 30-day period has expired.  In
doing so, subtract 61 days (September 20, 1982, through November 21,
1982) from the number of days since you first received the tickets

Page Two
December 9, 1982


(in counting the number of elapsed days, do not count the day you
received the tickets, but do count the day you returned or paid for
them).

If you received USC basketball season tickets, they are valued at
$100.00 each for a total of $292.00 for four of them. However, the
$250.00 limit on gifts would apply to the combined compliment of
football and basketball season tickets received from USC.

Payments for the latter should be made within 30 days of the re-
ceipt of same and, if paying by check, should be made payable to
University of Southern California.

If you have any questions, please call.

Sincerely,

LOS ANGELES COLISEUM COMMISSION


(Mrs.) Margaret Farnum
Assistant Secretary

M J FRANKOVICH

LOS ANGELES MEMORIAL COLISEUM COMMISSION

JOHN FERRARO
IRVING ZEIGER
ALTERNATES

COLISEUM, 3911 SO. FIGUEROA ST. • 747-7111 — SPORTS ARENA, 3939 S. FIGUEROA ST. • 748-6131
LOS ANGELES, CALIFORNIA 90037

September 10, 1982

TO:     Los Angeles Coliseum Commissioners,
        Alternates, Attorneys, General Manager

FROM:   Margaret Farnum

RE:     Complimentary Tickets for 1982 Football Season


        Under the Conflict of Interest and Disclosure Code, pursuant to
the Political Reform Act of 1974, public officials who receive
gifts from any one person or organization in excess of $250.00
may not participate in any business or decisions involving that
person or organization.

        The value of a 1982 Raiders' football season ticket is $180.00.
Four season tickets would amount to $720.00, and two tickets would
be valued at $360.00, both of which are in excess of $250.00.

        You may wish to (1) return tickets to our office to decrease the
amount to $250.00, (2) send a check to the Los Angeles Raiders, in
care of our office, in the amount of $110.00 (for two tickets) or
$470.00 (for four tickets), or (3) a combination of the above.
If tickets are returned, they should be well in advance of the
game.

        The value of a 1982 USC football season ticket is $76.00. Four
season tickets are valued at $304.00. It is my suggestion that
those who received four season tickets as gifts may wish to send
a check to our office, made payable to University of Southern
California, in the amount of $54.00 to bring you within the proper
gift amount. Because we do not handle the distribution of USC's
football tickets, we cannot handle the return of tickets.

        If you have any questions, please call (747-7111).


        [illegible]

CITY HALL OFFICE
465-3343

DISTRICT OFFICE
18917 NORDHOFF ST. STE 18
NORTHRIDGE 91324
989-2501
886.5210

City Council
of the
City of Los Angeles
City Hall
90012

HAL BERNSON
COUNCILMAN
TWELFTH DISTRICT

COMMITTEES:
CHAIRMAN,
GRANTS, HOUSING AND
COMMUNITY DEVELOPMENT

MEMBER
INTERGOVERNMENTAL RELATIONS
PLANNING AND ENVIRONMENT

December 15, 1982

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Gentlemen:

A question has been raised regarding the receipt of tickets from the Los Angeles
Raiders football team by my office. I would appreciate your ruling on whether or
not I now have a conflict of interest in matters dealing with the Los Angeles
Memorial Coliseum and the Los Angeles Raiders.

On September 1, 1982, my office received four season tickets to the Raiders games
which were scheduled to be played in the Los Angeles Memorial Coliseum. (The City
of Los Angeles is a one-third partner with the County of Los Angeles and the State
of California in the administration of the Coliseum.) The first tickets were for
a game played on August 29, 1982, three days prior to receipt.

The tickets for the games to be played on December 18, and December 26, 1982, have
not been used and have been returned.

The games scheduled for October 3, 10, 31, and November 7, 1982, were cancelled
because of the NFL players' strike.

The tickets for the games played on September 4, November 22, and December 5, 1982,
were given to constituents and I have subsequently sent a check to pay for them.

My request is, "Does the conflict of interest occur upon receipt of the tickets
(September 1), or for the use of tickets i.e., September 4, November 22, and
December 5, which value is $216.00, under the $250.00 limitation?" The balance
of tickets were returned to the Raiders on December 15, 1982, with a check for
$216.00.

Sincerely,

[signature]

HAL BERNSON
Councilman, 12th District

sections.question:
My request is, "Does the conflict of interest 
occur upon receipt of the tickets (September 1), or for 
the use of ticKets, i.e., September 4, November 22, and 
December 5, wnich value is $216.00, under the $250 
limitation?" 
The balance of tickets were returned to 
the Raiders on December 15, 1982, with a check for 
$216.
sections.conclusion:
to the precise facts of this situation. 
The 
Raiders were required Dy tne lease agreement to supply the 
236 tickets. 
However, the Coliseum exercised all the 
discretionary control over the distribution of these 
tickets. 
The Coliseum decided which tickets, and how many 
of them, went to each individual official. 
Consequently, 
as per our previous advice to Mr. Alperin, the Coliseum is 
to be considered the source of the tickets in this 
situation. 
The Raiders merely fulfilled a contractual 
ooligation wnich was placed upon them by tne Coliseum as 
part of the overall contract. 
Had the Ra~ders wished to 
donate complimentary tickets to various officials, they 
clearly nad a sufficient allotment of their own from which 
to do so. 
In the future, if discretion as to the recipients of 
these complimentary tickets is vested in the Raiders (or 
any otller lessee), then they are the donor and the tiCkets 
are a gift. 
In that situation, receipt of $250 or more in 
tiCkets which are neither returned nor paid for within 30 
days would create t11e basis for a conflict of interest 7/ 
on tne part of a puolic official who receives such a gift. 
I trust that this letter has satisfactorily responded to 
your question.
sections.facts:
A question has been raised regarding the receipt 
of tickets from the Los Angeles Raiders football team 
by my office. 
I would appreciate your ruling on 
whether or not I now have a conflict of interest in 
matters dealing with the Los Angeles Me~orial Coliseum 
and the Los Angeles Raiders. 
On September 1, 1982, my office received four 
season tickets to the Raiders games which were 
scheduled to be played in the Los Angeles Memorial 
Coliseum. 
(The City of Los Angeles is a one-third 
partner with the County of Los Angeles and the State of 
California in the adminis tra tion of the Coliseum.) 
The 
first tickets were for a game played on August 29, 
1982, three days prior to receipt. 


Hal Bernson 
January 19, 1983  
Tne ticKets for the gdmes to be played on 
December 18, and Decemoer 26, 1982, nave not been used 
and have been returned. 
The games scheduled for October 3, 10, 31 and 
November 7, 1982, were cancelled because of the NFL 
players' strike. 
Tne tickets for the games played on September 4, 
November 22, and December 5, 1982, were given to 
constituents and I have subsequently sent a check to 
pay fo r them.
sections.analysis:
Generally, the Commission's advice has been that if 
free tickets or passes are received by city officials to 
events held at a municipal stadium where the seats were 
reserved for the city's use as a part of the stadium lease 
agreement, then there is no gift made to the officials 
because the ticKets come from the city and are merely part 
of the "compensation" for the official. 
This advice was 
originally given in 1979 in response to a request from 
John W. Witt, City Attorney for San Diego. 
When Mr. Alperin contacted us in July, we were advised 
that the Los ill1geles Coliseum Commission (hereinafter the 
"Coliseum") operates the Los Angeles Memorial Coliseum as a 
joint powers agency between the City of Los Angeles, the 
County of Los Angeles and the State of California (the 6th 
Agricultural District, which is the Museum of Science and 
Industry). 
As part of its lease agreements, the Coliseum 
routinely requires the lessee to set aside a certain number 
of tickets to the lessee's events which are then distri-
buted according to a list prepared by the Coliseum. 
The 
list consists of public officials who receive either two or 
four tickets to each event, depending on how many the 


Hal Bernson 
January 19, 1983  
Coliseum decides to allocate to each specific official. 
The tickets are then provided and distributed by the lessee 
to this list of public officials. 
We were told to assume 
that the lessee has no veto power over the selections,ll 
out does have discretion over seat 10cation.~1 
We advised Mr. Alperin that, since the Coliseum is a 
triumvirate comprised of the City, County and State, the 
free tickets to City officials would be deemed to be part 
of the compensation which they received from the City. 
The 
same is true for County officials as well as for City and 
County staff actually assigned to the Coliseum. 
However, 
for state officials, only those who are actually members of 
the Coliseum, would fall into this category, along with 
state staff assigned to the Coliseum. 
As compensation from 
a public entity, the tickets do not represent reportable 
income and, hence, raise no conflict of interest problems. 
In response to our inquiries following receipt of your 
letter, we are now advised oy Mr. Alperin and his 
colleague, Tom Bonaventura, the deputy city attorney 
assigned to the Coliseum, of the following additional 
facts. 
1. 
The Coliseum adopted a policy in August 1~73 
relative to the issue of complimentary tickets to be 
distributed by lessees for Coliseum events. 
(See Exhibit 
"A".) 
General complimentary tickets to be distributed at 
the sole discretion of the lessee are limited to a maximum 
of 3,000 per event. 
This is to protect the Coliseum's 
interests since its revenues are based upon a 
II Mr. Alperin indicated that it was possible that 
the Coliseum merely prepares a "shopping list" of names of 
public officials, and the lessee may then select among 
those names in determining who will receive the free 
tickets (some 200+). 
Given that this was a speculative 
version of the facts, we advised Mr. Alperin on the basis 
of the assumption that the Coliseum actually selects the 
recipients of the ticKets. 
~I In point of fact, you have received "vpry good" 
seats according to the Coliseum. 
1 of the 226 seats 
provided by the Raiders are in good locations. 


Hal Bernson 
January 19, 1983  
percentage of the gate receipts. 
In addition to the 
foregoing allotment, the policy provides for a maximum of 
340 additional complimentary tickets to be distributed by 
the lessee to local governmental officials. 
(See Exhioit 
"A".) 
A list is provided oy the Coliseum. 
The list 
consists of local pUblic officials, including Coliseum 
staff, and assorted political figures. 
Each official is 
alloted either four tickets or two tickets, according to 
the list. 
As new officials are elected or appointed to 
positions, the names on the list change. 
The list is 
periodically reviewed and revised by the Coliseum. 
Currently, tne revised list totals 318, although at one 
point it was down to 300. 
2. 
Botn of the limits for complimentary tickets 
contained in tne Coliseum policy are negotiable in the 
lease agreements. 
For instance, when the Rams were the 
lessee, one year they wanted to cut back on the number of 
complimentary tickets which they were required to hand out 
to public officials and politicians. 
In the preli~inary 
lease contract ("Agreement" - see Exhibit "B") entered into 
between the Coliseum and the Raiders for the 1982 season, 
the Raiders negotiated the number of required complimentary 
tickets down to 236. 
The Agreement calls for the Raiders 
to provide to the Coliseum 236 tickets for each Raiders 
horne game. 
The tickets shall be provided in such locations 
as the Raioers may determine; however, once the 150 suites 
are constructed by the Raiders, three suites shall be 
included in the allotment to the Coliseum. 
3. 
The agreement between the Raiders and the Coliseum" 
was reached in July 1982, and the pre-season games were 
scneduled to begin in August. 
Consequently, time frames 
were very short and ticket problems were prevalent. 
Ms. Margaret Farnum, Assistant Secretary to the Coliseum, 
had several conversations with Mr. Al LoCasale, a chief 
Raiders assistant, regarding the need for tne Raiders to 
make gooo on their contractual obligation to provide the 
complimentary tickets for the public officials in a timely 
fashion. 
Finally, Mr. LoCasale advised Ms. Farnum that the 
Raiders could not accomplish the task of distributing these 
tickets. 
He then delivered a box to Ms. Farnum's office 
containing 236 sets of season tickets to all of the 
scneduled horne games of the Raiders. 
The task of 


Hal Bernson 
January 19, 1983  
distribution was turned over to her assistant, who pared 
down the list of 318 to 236 and distributed the tickets to 
that selected group,l/ first removing one ticKet for an 
exhibition game which had already Deen played. 
You were 
one of the recipients of these tickets. 
Ms. Farnum's 
assistant, with her input, determined the location of the 
tickets distributed to the various recipients. 
ANALYSIS 
You received the tickets on Septmeber 1, 1982. 
The 
value of them is the fair marKet value (see our HopKins 
~pinion, 3 FPPC Opinions 107, No. 77-022, December 8, 1977, 
copy enclosed). 
In the case of these Raiders tickets, that 
is $18 eacn. 
However, since the August 29, 1982, game was 
played prior to receipt of the tickets by you, the value of 
tickets to that game will not be counted in the total. 
At 
the time you received the ticKets, there were nine home 
games remaining on the Raiders sChedule. 
Thus the value of 
the ticKets received was 9 x 4 x $18, which equals $648. 
However, because of the intervening strike, that value was 
reduced to $360. 
Therefore, if the ticKets were a 
1/ 
Some of the recipients returned the tickets, 
unused, within 30 days of receipt. 
Ms. Farnum kept these 
tickets for others on the list who hadn't received any. 
Unclaimed tiCkets were distributed to Coliseum staff, none 
were returned to the Raiders. 
Some recipients paid for all 
or a portion of the tickets within 30 days of receipt. 
Checks were made payable to the Raiders, but were sent to 
Ms. Farnum, whO then forwarded them to tne Raiders. 
She 
had advised at least some of the recipients that the checKs 
should be made payable to the Raiders, because she assumed 
that tne Raiders were the donors. 
Written communications 
were sent by the Coliseum to its members and staff and 
sUbsequently to all recipients. 
These are attached as 
Exhibit "C". 
The advice and arithmetic contained therein 
is not necessarily accurate. 


Hal Bernson 
January 19, 1983  
giftil from the Raiders, both the disclosure~1 and 
disqualificationil provisions of the Act would be 
applicable. 
Under the circumstances, we do not think that the 
Raiders were a donor of the ticKets. 
We limit our 
il Government Code Section 82028, which defines the 
term "gift" for purposes of the Political Reform Act, 
provides, in part, that: 
"Gift" means, except as provided in sUbdivision 
(b), any payment to the extent that consideration of 
equal or greater value is not received and includes a 
rebate or discount in the price of anything of value 
unless the reoate or discount is made in the regular 
course of business to memoers of the puolic without 
regard to official status . ... 
Since you did not return the ticKets within 30 days, tne 
exception in Section 82028 (see below) does not apply. 
(b) 
The term "gift" does not include:

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: My request is, "Does the conflict of interest 
occur upon receipt of the tickets (September 1), or for 
the use of ticKets, i.e., September 4, November 22, and 
December 5, wnich value is $216.00, under the $250 
limitation?" 
The balance of tickets were returned to 
the Raiders on December 15, 1982, with a check for 
$216.

CONCLUSION: to the precise facts of this situation. 
The 
Raiders were required Dy tne lease agreement to supply the 
236 tickets. 
However, the Coliseum exercised all the 
discretionary control over the distribution of these 
tickets. 
The Coliseum decided which tickets, and how many 
of them, went to each individual official. 
Consequently, 
as per our previous advice to Mr. Alperin, the Coliseum is 
to be considered the source of the tickets in this 
situation. 
The Raiders merely fulfilled a contractual 
ooligation wnich was placed upon them by tne Coliseum as 
part of the overall contract. 
Had the Ra~ders wished to 
donate complimentary tickets to various officials, they 
clearly nad a sufficient allotment of their own from which 
to do so. 
In the future, if discretion as to the recipients of 
these complimentary tickets is vested in the Raiders (or 
any otller lessee), then they are the donor and the tiCkets 
are a gift. 
In that situation, receipt of $250 or more in 
tiCkets which are neither returned nor paid for within 30 
days would create t11e basis for a conflict of interest 7/ 
on tne part of a puolic official who receives such a gift. 
I trust that this letter has satisfactorily responded to 
your question.
embedding.first_500_words: State of California Fair Political Practices Commission P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest (916) 322-5662 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441 322-6444 January 26, 1983 Norman Flete Deputy Attorney General Department of Justice 3580 Wilshire Blvd. Los Angeles, CA 90010 Re: Los Angeles Coliseum - Raiders Tickets Our File No. A-82-211 Dear Mr. Flete: You have requested a clarification of one portion of our recent letter to Los Angeles City Councilman Hal Bernson (No. A-82-211). You are the Deputy Attorney General assigned to advise the State's participants on the Coliseum Commission. You have provided the following supplemental facts: The State's portion of the triumvirate Coliseum Commission ("Coliseum") is the 6th Agricultural District (The California Museum of Science and Industry). The Board of the 6th Agricultural District is appointed by the Governor to fixed terms. This Board, in turn, selects from its own membership its representatives on the Coliseum. I have advised you that, based upon these facts, the officials of the 6th Agricultural District would be treated the same as city and county officials.* Thus, the distribution of free tickets to the Raiders games, by the Coliseum (as described in the Bernson letter) to 6th Agricultural District officials would be considered part of their compensation and thus not *Officials here include all high-ranking city and county officials, such as treasurer, city attorney, county counsel, etc. Norman Fleete January 26, 1983 Page Two gift. Contrary to what the Benson letter stated, this is not limited to the 6th Agricultural District officials who actually sit on the Coliseum Commission. I trust that this clarifies the matter to your satisfaction. Sincerely, Robert E. Leidigh Counsel Legal Division REL:plh cc: Anthony Saul Alperin Thomas Bonaventura Deputy City Attorneys State of California Fair Political Practices Commission P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest (916) 322-5662 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441 322-6444 January 19, 1983 Hal Bernson Councilmember, 12th District City of Los Angeles City Hall Los Angeles, CA 90012 Re: Your Request for Written Advice, Our No. A-82-211 Dear Councilmember Bernson: Thank you for your recent letter requesting our advice regarding complimentary tickets to Raiders football games at the Los Angeles Coliseum. We have previously received telephone inquiries from Anthony Saul Alperin of the City Attorney's Office regarding the issue and have rendered advice to him on the subject. However, it was unclear to him at that time who, in fact, was the provider of the tickets. You state the facts in your letter as follows: FACTS A question has been raised regarding the receipt of tickets from the Los Angeles Raiders football team by my office. I would appreciate your ruling on whether or not I now have a conflict of interest in matters dealing with the Los
